16 FE5: year SRR_median 0.992681 0.420627 2.360002 0.018275 0.168267 1.817095 0.102804 0.075965 222 965
17 FE5: year SRR_mean 1.036224 0.435375 2.380070 0.017309 0.182904 1.889544 0.102931 0.076095 222 965
18 FE5: year normalized_SRR_mean 165.682358 70.134503 2.362352 0.018159 28.221257 303.143458 0.102792 0.075953 222 965
19 FE5: year normalized_SRR_median 166.483325 70.258864 2.369570 0.017809 28.778482 304.188169 0.102845 0.076007 222 965

The association between SRR and next-year acquisition size is not robust for the raw SRR measures.

Overall, SRR is most strongly related to deal size in next-year models

4. Robustness check¶

To check whether the SRR–M&A relationship still hold when we make reasonable alternative assumptions

4.1 Winsorize SRR only among observations with transaction volume at 2%¶

Purpose: to mitigate the influence of extreme transaction values and ensure that estimated relationships are not driven by a small number of unusually large or small deals, we trim the transaction size distribution by excluding observations below the 2nd percentile and above the 98th percentile. This improves the stability of OLS estimates and provides a robustness check that the SRR–deal size association holds for the bulk of the sample

Result: normalized_SRR is positively and significantly associated with contemporaneous acquisition size. The estimate remains stable and statistically significant after controlling for firm and CEO characteristics and when including sector fixed effects

In [68]:
#remove outlier at the smallest 2% and largest 2%

q2 = final_data["transactionsize_usd"].quantile(0.02)
q98 = final_data["transactionsize_usd"].quantile(0.98)

final_data_filtered = final_data[(final_data["transactionsize_usd"] >= q2) &
                    (final_data["transactionsize_usd"] <= q98)]

# Create 2 subplots side by side
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 5))  # 1 row, 2 columns

# --- Before filtered ---
axes[0].scatter(final_data["gsector"], final_data["transactionsize_usd"],
                alpha=0.3, s=10)
axes[0].set_xlabel("GICS Sector (gsector)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Transaction size")
axes[0].set_title("Before Filter")
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

# --- After filtered ---
axes[1].scatter(final_data_filtered["gsector"], final_data_filtered["transactionsize_usd"],
                alpha=0.3, s=10)
axes[1].set_xlabel("GICS Sector (gsector)")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Transaction size")
axes[1].set_title("After Filter")
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [69]:
# --- filter outliers (2% / 98%) ---
q2  = final_data["log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear"].quantile(0.02)
q98 = final_data["log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear"].quantile(0.98)

final_data_filtered = final_data[
    (final_data["log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear"] >= q2) &
    (final_data["log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear"] <= q98)
].copy()

# --- helper: fit with firm-clustered SE ---
def fit_clustered(formula, data, cluster_col="gvkey"):
    base = smf.ols(formula, data=data, missing="drop")
    used_idx = base.data.row_labels
    groups = data.loc[used_idx, cluster_col]
    return base.fit(cov_type="cluster", cov_kwds={"groups": groups, "use_correction": True})

# 1) Simple model (clustered)
m1 = fit_clustered("log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear ~ normalized_SRR_median", final_data_filtered)

# 2) Controls (clustered)
m2 = fit_clustered(
    "log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear ~ normalized_SRR_median + TobinQ + tenure_years + gender "
    "+ log_stock_awards + EBIT_Margin + shrown_excl_opts_pct "
    "+ Leverage + log_option_awards + log_revt_lag + log_at_lag",
    final_data_filtered
)


# 3) Sector FE (clustered)
m3 = fit_clustered(
    "log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear ~ normalized_SRR_median + TobinQ + tenure_years + gender "
    "+ log_stock_awards + EBIT_Margin + shrown_excl_opts_pct "
    "+ Leverage + log_option_awards + log_revt_lag + log_at_lag + C(gsector)",
    final_data_filtered
)

# --- Compare coef + clustered SE + p-value ---
print("\n==========================================")
print(f"1. Simple:        coef={m1.params['normalized_SRR_median']:.3f}  se={m1.bse['normalized_SRR_median']:.3f}  p={m1.pvalues['normalized_SRR_median']:.3g}")
print(f"2. + Controls:    coef={m2.params['normalized_SRR_median']:.3f}  se={m2.bse['normalized_SRR_median']:.3f}  p={m2.pvalues['normalized_SRR_median']:.3g}")
print(f"3. + Sector FE:   coef={m3.params['normalized_SRR_median']:.3f}  se={m3.bse['normalized_SRR_median']:.3f}  p={m3.pvalues['normalized_SRR_median']:.3g}")
print("------------------------------------------")
print("==========================================")

# Optional: full summaries
# print(m1.summary()); print(m2.summary()); print(m3.summary())

==========================================
1. Simple:        coef=128.932  se=58.944  p=0.0287
2. + Controls:    coef=169.829  se=67.840  p=0.0123
3. + Sector FE:   coef=174.912  se=62.522  p=0.00515
------------------------------------------
==========================================

4.2 Lag corrected SRR by 1 year (Causality)¶

Result: No causality is seen

In [70]:
# sort panel
final_data = final_data.sort_values(["gvkey", "publish_year"]).copy()

# 1-year lag of SRR measures (within firm)

final_data["normalized_SRR_mean_lag1"] = final_data.groupby("gvkey")["normalized_SRR_mean"].shift(1)
final_data["normalized_SRR_median_lag1"] = final_data.groupby("gvkey")["normalized_SRR_median"].shift(1)

# Helper: OLS with FE + firm-clustered SEs
def fit_fe_clustered(formula, data, cluster_col="gvkey"):
    base = smf.ols(formula, data=data, missing="drop")
    used_idx = base.data.row_labels
    groups = data.loc[used_idx, cluster_col]
    return base.fit(cov_type="cluster", cov_kwds={"groups": groups, "use_correction": True})

# Model with controls + FE
# # Firm + year FE
m_lag_firm = fit_fe_clustered(
    "log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear ~ normalized_SRR_mean_lag1 + TobinQ + tenure_years + gender "
    "+ age + log_stock_awards + EBIT_Margin + shrown_excl_opts_pct + Leverage "
    "+ log_option_awards + log_revt_lag + log_at_lag "
    "+ C(gsector) + C(publish_year)",
    final_data
)

print(m_lag_firm.summary())

print("\n==========================================")

var = "normalized_SRR_mean_lag1"

print("coef :", m_lag_firm.params[var])
print("se   :", m_lag_firm.bse[var])
print("pval :", m_lag_firm.pvalues[var])

In [71]:
# sort panel
final_data = final_data.sort_values(["gvkey", "publish_year"]).copy()

# 1-year lag of SRR measures (within firm)

final_data["normalized_SRR_mean_lag1"] = final_data.groupby("gvkey")["normalized_SRR_mean"].shift(1)
final_data["normalized_SRR_median_lag1"] = final_data.groupby("gvkey")["normalized_SRR_median"].shift(1)

# Helper: OLS with FE + firm-clustered SEs
def fit_fe_clustered(formula, data, cluster_col="gvkey"):
    base = smf.ols(formula, data=data, missing="drop")
    used_idx = base.data.row_labels
    groups = data.loc[used_idx, cluster_col]
    return base.fit(cov_type="cluster", cov_kwds={"groups": groups, "use_correction": True})

# Model with controls + FE
# # Firm + year FE
m_lag_firm = fit_fe_clustered(
    "normalized_SRR_mean_lag1 ~ log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear+ TobinQ + tenure_years + gender "
    "+ age + log_stock_awards + EBIT_Margin + shrown_excl_opts_pct + Leverage "
    "+ log_option_awards + log_revt_lag + log_at_lag "
    "+ C(gsector) + C(publish_year)",
    final_data
)

print(m_lag_firm.summary())

print("\n==========================================")

var = "log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear"

print("coef :", m_lag_firm.params[var])
print("se   :", m_lag_firm.bse[var])
print("pval :", m_lag_firm.pvalues[var])

In [74]:
df = final_data.sort_values(["gvkey", "publish_year"]).copy()

# Lead of Y (t+1) within firm
df["log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear"] = (
    df.groupby("gvkey")["log_transaction_size_usd"].shift(-1)
)

# Lag of Y (t)
# If you want explicit lag:
df["log_transaction_size_usd_lag1"] = (
    df.groupby("gvkey")["log_transaction_size_usd"].shift(0)  # same-year
)

# Lag of SRR (t-1 relative to nextyear is t)
# If your normalized_SRR_mean_lag1 is already computed correctly, skip this.
df["normalized_SRR_mean_lag1"] = (
    df.groupby("gvkey")["normalized_SRR_mean"].shift(0)  # same-year relative to nextyear
)

# Drop rows where nextyear is missing (last year per firm) + any missing regressors
needed = [
    "log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear",
    "log_transaction_size_usd",
    "normalized_SRR_mean_lag1",
    "TobinQ", "tenure_years", "gender", "age",
    "log_stock_awards", "EBIT_Margin", "shrown_excl_opts_pct", "Leverage",
    "log_option_awards", "log_revt_lag", "log_at_lag",
    "gsector", "publish_year", "gvkey"
]
d = df.dropna(subset=needed).copy()


# Unrestricted: add SRR lag
f_unrestricted = (
    "log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear ~ "
    "log_transaction_size_usd "
    "+ normalized_SRR_mean_lag1 "
    "+ TobinQ + tenure_years + gender + age "
    "+ log_stock_awards + EBIT_Margin + shrown_excl_opts_pct + Leverage "
    "+ log_option_awards + log_revt_lag + log_at_lag "
    "+ C(gsector) + C(publish_year)"
)

m_u = smf.ols(f_unrestricted, data=d).fit(cov_type="cluster",
                                      cov_kwds={"groups": d["gvkey"]})


print(m_u.summary())
print("coef SRR:", m_u.params["normalized_SRR_mean_lag1"])
print("pval SRR:", m_u.pvalues["normalized_SRR_mean_lag1"])
print("coef lagged log_transaction_size_usd:", m_u.params["log_transaction_size_usd"])
print("pval lagged log_transaction_size_usd:", m_u.pvalues["log_transaction_size_usd"])

                                    OLS Regression Results

There is no evidence for SRR Granger-causes deal size next year (p=0.296).

4.3 Winsorize SRR only among observations with transaction volume at 1%¶

In [75]:
def winsorize_series(s, p_low=0.01, p_high=0.99):
    lo, hi = s.quantile([p_low, p_high])
    return s.clip(lower=lo, upper=hi)
mask = final_data["transactionsize_usd_nextyear"].notna()


final_data.loc[mask, "normalized_SRR_mean_w"] = winsorize_series(final_data.loc[mask, "normalized_SRR_mean"], 0.01, 0.99)
final_data.loc[mask, "normalized_SRR_median_w"] = winsorize_series(final_data.loc[mask, "normalized_SRR_median"], 0.01, 0.99)

#Regression with basic controls
control_vars = ["TobinQ", "tenure_years", "gender", "age", "log_stock_awards", "log_stock_awards",
                "EBIT_Margin","shrown_excl_opts_pct","Leverage","log_option_awards","log_revt_lag","log_at_lag"]
model_with_controls, models_basic = ols_with_controls_clustered(
    data=final_data,
    dependent_var="log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear",
    independent_vars=["normalized_SRR_mean_w", "normalized_SRR_median_w"],
    control_vars=control_vars,
)   
print("Regression with Winsorized SRR Controls Results on log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear with Controls:\n")
display(model_with_controls)

Regression with Winsorized SRR Controls Results on log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear with Controls:
Independent_Variable coef Std_Error T_Stat P_Value [0.025 0.975] R_Squared Adj_R_Squared N_Clusters
0 normalized_SRR_mean_w 353.064675 114.935280 3.071856 0.002127 127.795665 578.333685 0.09469 0.083278 222
1 normalized_SRR_median_w 355.463552 114.805733 3.096218 0.001960 130.448449 580.478655 0.09480 0.083390 222

Across all 2 SRR measures, the coefficient stays positive and statistically significant after winsorization and controls. The SRR–deal-size link is not just caused by a few outlier interviews.

In [76]:
# ---- inputs ----

models_fe = ols_with_controls_and_fe_clusterd(
    data=final_data,
    dependent_var="log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear",
    independent_vars=["normalized_SRR_mean_w", "normalized_SRR_median_w"],
    control_vars=control_vars,
    fe_specs=fe_specs,
    cov_type="nonrobust"
)

results_df = fe_results_table(models_fe, data=final_data, cluster_col="gvkey")
print("Regression with Winsorized SRR Controls and Fixed Effects Results on log_transaction_size_usd_nextyear:\n")
display(results_df)

2 FE2: sector + year normalized_SRR_mean_w 382.594141 135.222584 2.829366 0.004765 117.215881 647.972400 0.128296 0.092524 222 965
3 FE2: sector + year normalized_SRR_median_w 385.343439 134.996836 2.854463 0.004407 120.408217 650.278661 0.128429 0.092662 222 965
4 FE3: firm normalized_SRR_mean_w 347.958907 146.313680 2.378171 0.017655 60.713766 635.204048 0.414278 0.227584 222 965
5 FE3: firm normalized_SRR_median_w 348.241984 146.282764 2.380608 0.017539 61.057540 635.426429 0.414287 0.227596 222 965
6 FE4: sector + firm normalized_SRR_mean_w 347.958907 146.313680 2.378171 0.017655 60.713766 635.204048 0.414278 0.227584 222 965
7 FE4: sector + firm normalized_SRR_median_w 348.241984 146.282764 2.380608 0.017539 61.057540 635.426429 0.414287 0.227596 222 965
8 FE5: year normalized_SRR_mean_w 364.240113 134.679747 2.704491 0.006965 99.930882 628.549345 0.104854 0.078076 222 965
9 FE5: year normalized_SRR_median_w 366.481840 134.487971 2.725016 0.006550 102.548970 630.414710 0.104960 0.078185 222 965

The estimated association between normalized SRR and next-year acquisition size remains positive and statistically significant across multiple fixed-effect specifications.

4.4 Check multicollinearity¶

Purpose:To assess whether correlation among regressors inflates estimation uncertainty, we compute variance inflation factors (VIFs) for the main explanatory and control variables.

Intuition: High VIF values indicate strong multicollinearity, which can increase standard errors and produce unstable coefficient estimates across specifications.

Result: Variance inflation factors (VIFs) for the main explanatory variable and all controls are low (all < 2.1), indicating that multicollinearity is not a material concern

In [77]:
# Check multicollinearity by VIF

def compute_vif(df, features):
    X = df[features].assign(constant=1)  # add constant for intercept
    vif_data = pd.DataFrame()
    vif_data["feature"] = features
    vif_data["VIF"] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(len(features))]
    return vif_data
features = [
    "normalized_SRR_mean", "TobinQ", "tenure_years", "gender", "CF", "age", "Aqc_Ratio",
    "shrown_excl_opts_pct", "revt_lag", "Leverage",
    "log_option_awards", "log_stock_awards", "opt_unex_exer_est_val"
]
vif_results = compute_vif(final_data, features)
print(vif_results)

                  feature       VIF
0     normalized_SRR_mean  1.008127
1                  TobinQ  1.472676
2            tenure_years  1.788360
3                  gender  1.055481
4                      CF  1.291133
5                     age  1.524452
6               Aqc_Ratio  1.083126
7    shrown_excl_opts_pct  2.076961
8                revt_lag  1.098426
9                Leverage  1.045056
10      log_option_awards  1.142242
11       log_stock_awards  1.361907
12  opt_unex_exer_est_val  1.434540

Insight¶

According to our model transaction next year, we find a positive relationship between the level of CEO narcisism (SRR normalized mean and median) and the transaction size (significant at level 5%). The significance hold under 5% in single regression, muliple regression and also when putting fix effect to control for time invariant variables or when remove 0.1% outlier in the data

The model same year regression and next 2 year regression show the relationship between SRR and transaction size is not significant

SRR is statistical significant and positive associated with the acquisition activity next year

In the regression, we also find that some control factor are significantly with firm acquisition Three variables emerge as highly significant predictors:

  1. Organizational Scale: Lagged total assets (log_at_lag) confirms that a firm’s existing balance sheet capacity is the strongest anchor for future deal size or large firm are about to conduct more merge and aquisition
  2. Operating Efficiency: The EBIT_Margin shows a robust positive association. This suggests that highly profitable firms leverage their superior cash-flow generation to execute larger-scale acquisitions.
  3. Growth Opportunities: Tobin’s Q remains a significant positive driver, indicating that firms with high market-to-book valuations—often interpreted as having a "mandate for growth"—consistently pursue larger targets.

Interaction effect of SRR and sector show that The coefficent is significantly more positive in Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, IT, Communication Service, Real Estate, Healthcare. As SRR increases, the change in transaction size is higher for Industrial, Comsumer Discretionary, IT, Real Estate and Healthcare than Energy as base Sector

The model demonstrates that traditional governance and demographic variables have negligible predictive power regarding transaction size

  1. CEO Characteristics: Neither gender nor tenure_years impact the scale of transactions.
  2. Compensation Structures: The magnitude of stock awards and option awards does not appear to incentivize larger deal tickets.
  3. Capital Structure: Current leverage does not act as a statistically significant constraint on the following year's acquisition size.

Reference¶

Liu, Yue (Lucy) and Taffler, Richard J., Damned Out of Their Own Mouths: CEO Narcissism in M&A Decision-Making and Its Impact on Firm Performance (February 15, 2009). Available at SSRN: https://ssrn.com/abstract=1344119 or http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.1344119
